summary: Gruber promotes Dithering, his paid podcast with Ben Thompson, highlighting their dynamic of constructive disagreement. He notes their recent spirited debate over Apple's ban on Microsoft's Xbox Game Pass streaming service from the App Store. The post announces a new free preview podcast called Dithering Preview, designed to give prospective subscribers a taste of the show before committing to the $5/month fee. He frames the preview as a solution they'd been planning since the show's conception.
key_points: Gruber and Thompson's podcast strength comes from seeing issues from different perspectives, even when they agree
Their disagreement over Apple's Xbox Game Pass App Store ban has produced particularly strong episodes
A new free Dithering Preview podcast launches to address the discovery problem inherent in paid podcasts
Good intellectual disagreement requires a shared willingness to honestly contemplate being wrong
money_quotes: One reason Ben and I make for a good team, I think, is that even when we agree on something, we often see the issue from very different perspectives.
What makes for an invigorating disagreement is the shared belief that you can only have confidence that your opinion is correct if you're willing to honestly contemplate that you might be wrong.
Our arguments are constructive, not destructive.
The obvious downside to a paid subscription podcast ($5/month — cheap!) is that some prospective listeners naturally want to know what the show is like before paying.
If you've been on the fence, give an episode or two of Dithering Preview a listen, and you'll get an honest taste of what the show is like.
key_insight: The best intellectual partnerships thrive not on agreement but on constructive disagreement grounded in mutual willingness to be wrong.
